title: Primary Ovarian Insufficiency
chief_complaint: My periods have become irregular and now stopped completely for 6 months. I have hot flashes, night sweats, and vaginal dryness. I'm trying to get pregnant. My mother went through menopause at age 38.

physical_exam.General: Normal weight. No stigmata of Turner syndrome.
physical_exam.Pelvic: Normal external genitalia. Vagina: Pale, thin mucosa (atrophic changes from estrogen deficiency). Uterus normal size. Ovaries not palpable.

hidden_data.hormones: FSH 85 mIU/mL (markedly elevated - normal <10 follicular). Repeat FSH 78 (confirmed elevated). Estradiol <15 pg/mL (low - postmenopausal levels). AMH <0.01 ng/mL (undetectable - no ovarian reserve). LH 45 (elevated). Prolactin normal. TSH 2.5 (normal).
hidden_data.antral_follicle: TVUS: No antral follicles visualized in either ovary. Ovaries small (Right 1.5mL, Left 1.2mL). Uterus normal size. Endometrial stripe thin (3mm - no estrogen effect).
hidden_data.fragile_x: FMR1 premutation (55-200 CGG repeats) - most common genetic cause of POI.
hidden_data.adrenal: Adrenal antibodies negative (rules out autoimmune adrenal insufficiency - Addison disease).
hidden_data.thyroid: Thyroid peroxidase antibodies positive (Hashimoto thyroiditis - associated autoimmune condition).

correct_diagnosis: Primary Ovarian Insufficiency (POI) from FMR1 Premutation (Fragile X Carrier) with Associated Hashimoto Thyroiditis
key_learning_points: POI = loss of ovarian function before age 40. Formerly called 'premature ovarian failure' - term changed because 5-10% may spontaneously conceive. Incidence 1% of women. NOT menopause (which is permanent)

Causes: Genetic (most common - FMR1 premutation, Turner syndrome, galactosemia, BMP15, FOXL2), Autoimmune (APS types 1/2 - associated with Addison, hypothyroidism, hypoparathyroidism, candidiasis), Iatrogenic (chemotherapy, radiation, bilateral oophorectomy), Idiopathic (70-90%). FMR1 premutation = 5-10% of POI cases. Test for fragile X in all POI patients <40

Diagnosis: FSH >40 on 2 occasions >1 month apart + Amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea >4 months. Low estradiol. Undetectable AMH (antral follicles absent). Must differentiate from hypothalamic amenorrhea (low/normal FSH, low LH, reversible)

Long-term consequences: Infertility, Osteoporosis, Cardiovascular disease, Cognitive decline (dementia risk), Sexual dysfunction, Psychological distress (grief, depression). All from estrogen deficiency

Treatment: 1) HORMONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY (HRT): Estrogen replacement (Estradiol patch 0.1mg or oral Estradiol 1-2mg daily). Add Progestin if uterus present (Medroxyprogesterone 10mg ×12 days/month or continuous combined). Continue until average age of menopause (51 years). Reduces osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, cognitive decline, and vasomotor symptoms. 2) CALCIUM 1200mg + VITAMIN D 800-1000 IU daily. DEXA scan baseline. 3) FERTILITY: Egg donation (IVF with donor oocytes) is most successful option. Spontaneous pregnancy in 5-10% (not zero!). Embryo or oocyte cryopreservation if at-risk (FMR1 premutation) and not yet desired pregnancy. 4) SCREEN for associated autoimmune conditions (TSH, CBC, CMP, morning cortisol) annually. 5) PSYCHOLOGICAL SUPPORT: Grief counseling, support groups. POI diagnosis is devastating - address emotional needs
